context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Ideas and Ideologies of Progress and Modernization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is progress a western notion, which emerged during the Enlightenment or, can we trace the concept to earlier times, and to non-western political/social formations? Is it universal? What are the differences between the concepts of progress, modernization, development and planning? How did these concepts originate? How are they connected? Do different development planning traditions draw on different aspects of the notion of Modernity? What is the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity' in international development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i just need this reflection done tbh—can u give me a solid 700–800 word draft i can submit for the prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the assignment for you isn't my role, and submitting work that isn't yours would be academic dishonesty.

I'm here to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself. Let's start simple: when you hear the word 'progress,' what comes to mind? What does it mean to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly decline, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I should pick one element of the prompt to get them started with a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: idk, like society getting “better” over time ig. but can u just give me a strong thesis + topic sentences so i can build it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I can do — the thesis and structure need to come from you.

But your instinct is interesting: 'society getting better over time.' Here's a question worth sitting with — who decides what 'better' looks like? And does everyone across history and cultures agree on that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the writing work for them, this time by asking for a thesis and topic sentences. I must refuse clearly but redirect constructively. Their intuition about 'society getting better' is actually a decent starting point I can build on Socratically. I should push them to interrogate that idea, since the prompt specifically asks whether progress is a Western/Enlightenment notion or something more universal — that tension is at the heart of the assignment.

exchanges.student: probably not lol, elites/states/colonizers def shaped that. can u at least tell me the main difference between progress, modernization, development, and planning in super plain terms?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct on power — that'll be useful in your reflection.

Rather than me defining those terms, let's try this: do you think 'progress' and 'modernization' mean the same thing, or is there a difference? What would you say sets them apart?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the four key concepts directly, which would essentially hand them a core component of the assignment. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them discover the distinctions themselves. Their previous insight about power and who defines 'better' was good — I can build on that to help them think about how these four terms might differ. I'll ask a targeted question that nudges them toward distinguishing the concepts without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: nah not exactly—progress feels broader/normative, modernization is more like becoming industrial/bureaucratic? i just need a clean 1-line distinction for each tho fr
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid distinction — hold onto it, it could anchor part of your reflection.

Now apply the same thinking: how would 'development' and 'planning' fit relative to what you just said? Are they more like 'progress' or more like 'modernization,' or something else entirely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely good conceptual distinction between progress and modernization. I should affirm this and push them to extend the same reasoning to 'development' and 'planning' themselves, rather than giving them clean one-liners. Writing those one-liners is exactly the kind of work the student needs to do themselves for the assignment. I should keep them moving forward with a targeted question.

exchanges.student: development feels like the policy/econ version of “improvement,” and planning is the state strategy part. can u just say if that’s basically right and maybe fix the wording a bit?
exchanges.tutor:
Your thinking is heading in a good direction — those distinctions are worth developing further in your writing.

But the wording stays yours — that's your job, not mine.

Now, the prompt asks something trickier: is progress actually a Western, Enlightenment-era idea, or can you trace it elsewhere? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making reasonable distinctions and is now asking me to validate and polish their wording — the latter of which I must refuse, as editing or suggesting language for the student violates my rules. I should affirm the direction of their thinking without grading it, and importantly, not touch their wording. I should now push them toward the next key part of the prompt: the historical/geographical origins of these concepts, particularly the Western vs. non-Western question.

exchanges.student: i think Enlightenment made a specific version of it, but ppl def had ideas of improvement before that in China/Islamic empires n stuff. can u tell me if that argument is legit or too vague?
exchanges.tutor:
That argument has real potential — but you already sensed the weakness yourself: it's vague.

Can you think of one concrete example from China or the Islamic world that would back it up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a historically sound argument that aligns well with the prompt. I should affirm the direction without grading it, and push them to be more specific rather than vague — which is actually their own concern. Rather than telling them if it's 'legit,' I should ask them to support it with a concrete example, which will strengthen their reflection and move them toward more substantive content.

exchanges.student: maybe Confucian statecraft or Ibn Khaldun? ngl i don't remember enough details, so can u give me 2–3 concrete examples i could use?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are actually great instincts — both are highly relevant to the prompt.

You don't need me to fill in the details. What do you remember about either one, even roughly? Start there and we can build on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently surfaced two strong, relevant examples (Confucian statecraft and Ibn Khaldun) which is excellent. They're now asking me to provide concrete examples for them, which I must refuse — that's their work. Instead, I should affirm their instincts and push them to recall what they do know about one of those examples, rather than letting them outsource the research to me.

exchanges.student: tbh almost nothing rn. can u at least name the example + the one reason it matters, like super short?
exchanges.tutor:
That's research you need to do — it'll take 5 minutes to look up Ibn Khaldun or Confucian statecraft and find one key idea.

Check your course readings first. What does your syllabus or reading list say about this topic?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to provide content they should research themselves. I need to hold firm without being unhelpful. I can point them toward where to look without giving them the content. Ibn Khaldun and Confucian statecraft are topics they can quickly look up. I should redirect them to their course readings or a quick search, and remind them that finding this is part of their work.

exchanges.student: i don’t have it open lol. then just help me w/ the tradition vs modernity part—what’s the basic argument there?
exchanges.tutor:
Before we go further — you've actually built up a lot already: the four concepts, the Western vs. non-Western tension, Ibn Khaldun and Confucian statecraft as examples.

Quick question before tradition/modernity: do you think 'traditional' societies were static and unchanging, or is that assumption itself part of a Western narrative?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide content directly. I need to refuse but keep them engaged. We've now covered a lot of ground — progress vs. modernization/development/planning, Western vs. non-Western origins, concrete examples. The student has actually built up enough material to start writing. Given we're at exchange 8 of 10, and we've discussed several key components of the prompt, I should push them to start integrating what we've discussed into a written attempt rather than continuing to extract information. I should ask them a Socratic question about tradition vs. modernity but also nudge them toward writing.

exchanges.student: yeah that assumption is prob the problem. can u just give me one concise argument i can use for that section?
exchanges.tutor:
You just made the argument yourself — read your last message again.

You now have enough to start writing: the four concepts, the origins debate, your examples, and this insight on tradition. Stop extracting and start drafting. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just made a genuinely insightful point on their own — that the assumption of static 'traditional' societies is itself a Western narrative construct. That IS the argument. I should point this out to them directly so they realize they already have it, and push them to now start writing rather than continuing to extract content from me. We're at exchange 9 of 10, so I should strongly nudge them toward integrating everything into a draft.
